mnemonics.title: PAGING KEY CONCEPTS
mnemonics.content: FIVE P'S: Frames divide Physical memory, Pages divide Program memory, Each Page maps to a Frame, Paging Prevents external fragmentation, Present bit indicates if in memory.

mnemonics.title: PAGE FAULT HANDLING STEPS
mnemonics.content: PAGE FAULT = OS loads Page from Disk: 1) Check page table for validity, 2) Find free frame, 3) Read page from disk to frame, 4) Update page table, 5) Restart instruction.

mnemonics.title: MEMORY ALLOCATION STRATEGIES
mnemonics.content: FIRST IS FAST, BEST SAVES SPACE, WORST KEEPS LARGE HOLES: First-fit=fastest (search from beginning), Best-fit=least external fragmentation, Worst-fit=produces largest holes.

mnemonics.title: WORKING SET MEMORY MANAGEMENT
mnemonics.content: KEEP ACTIVE PAGES WORKING: Working set = pages process currently needs. If allocated frames < working set size, thrashing occurs. Increase frames to prevent.

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache storing recent page table entries for fast virtual-to-physical address translation.
Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using page tables.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the page least recently accessed when a new page is needed.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page in memory when a new page must be loaded.
Contiguous range of virtual addresses in a process with similar properties (read-only, executable, etc.).

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box split into two columns: LEFT = Virtual Address (Page# | Offset), RIGHT = Physical Address (Frame# | Offset). Draw an arrow from Page# pointing to Frame# going through a Page Table box in the middle.
Create a table with columns: Valid bit, Frame number, Protection bits. Show how each row maps one virtual page to one physical frame. Use checkmarks for valid=1, empty for valid=0.
INTERNAL: Draw a partitioned box with small unused space INSIDE each partition labeled 'wasted'. EXTERNAL: Draw scattered small boxes labeled 'free' between larger boxes labeled 'allocated'.
Draw a flowchart: CPU requests address -> Check TLB -> Hit? -> Use translation -> Miss? -> Access page table -> Update TLB -> Use translation. Highlight TLB as a 'shortcut' bridge.

analogies.analogy: Think of paging like a book borrowed from a library. You don't need the entire book at once - you only bring home the chapters (pages) you're currently reading. When you need a new chapter, you exchange (swap) it. You have limited shelf space (frames), but access to the entire library (virtual memory).
The page table is like a library catalog or index. It tells you which shelf (physical frame) contains which chapter (virtual page) of your book. Without it, you'd have to search every shelf.

TLB is like a frequently-used contacts list in your phone. Instead of searching the entire phonebook (page table in memory), you check your favorites first (TLB). Most of the time you find what you need quickly.

External fragmentation is like trying to park cars in a lot where cars have left gaps. Even though there are 5 empty spaces, none are big enough for a 6-car caravan. You need to move cars around (compaction) to make space.

analogies.anal analogy: Thrashing is like a student who takes out a book to read one sentence, puts it back, takes out another, reads one line, puts it back - spending more time fetching books than actually reading. The student is constantly swapping (thrashing) between books.
